chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The World of Metals and Non Metals

summary.category: Properties That Define Metals: Luster, Malleability, Ductility, Conductivity, and Sonority
points: Metals are distinguished by lustrous (shiny) appearance and unique properties: malleability (can be beaten into sheets like silver foil and aluminium foil), ductility (can be drawn into wires like copper in electrical systems), sonority (produce ringing sounds when struck like school bells), and excellent conduction of both heat (cooking vessels use metal for this) and electricity (wiring). In contrast, non-metals are non-lustrous, brittle, non-malleable, non-ductile, non-sonorous, and poor conductors. These defining properties explain why metals are chosen for specific applications and non-metals for others.

summary.category: Chemical Reactivity: Rusting, Corrosion, and Different Oxide Natures
points: Iron rusts (develops brown deposits) only when exposed to BOTH water AND air combined—neither alone causes rust. This leads to practical prevention methods: painting, oiling, or galvanization (zinc coating). Other metals corrode differently: copper develops green coating, silver develops black coating. Chemically, metals react with oxygen to form metal oxides that are basic in nature (turn red litmus blue), while non-metals like sulfur and phosphorus form non-metal oxides that are acidic in nature (turn blue litmus red). These chemical differences provide another way to distinguish metals from non-metals.

summary.category: Essential Non-metals in Life and Technology Despite Being Less Visible
points: While metals are prominent and visible (cooking vessels, tools, electrical wiring, structures), non-metals are often invisible yet equally or more essential: oxygen is essential for respiration, carbon is the foundation of all life forms, nitrogen is essential for plant growth through fertilizers, chlorine purifies water, and iodine protects wounds and health. Metals and non-metals together are elements—the building blocks of all matter—with 118 known elements having distinct properties and applications in everyday life, technology (titanium in aerospace, zirconium in nuclear energy), and sustainability efforts (recycling metals like iron and aluminium).
